entities.summary: Lists of citizens declared outlaw under Sulla in 82–81 BC; their lives forfeit and their property auctioned to the dictator's followers. Pro Roscio Amerino is the first oblique attack on the proscriptions in surviving Latin literature.

entities.canonical_name: Pompey's Games (dedication of his theatre, 55 BC)
entities.summary: The lavish games marking the dedication of Pompey's stone theatre in the Campus Martius, late September 55 BC: stage plays under Sp. Maecius Tarpa's curation, gladiators, two five-day venationes, ending with the famous slaughter of elephants whose distress drew the crowd's pity.
The great Roman public games of September, in honour of Jupiter Optimus Maximus. The setting of the De Oratore conversation, in 91 BC.
Festival celebrated by the Syracusans in honour of M. Claudius Marcellus and his line for sparing the city in 212 BC; abolished by Verres and replaced by the Verria.
Festival celebrated in the province of Asia in honour of Q. Mucius Scaevola the Pontifex (cos 95 BC); preserved even by Mithridates VI when he occupied the province in 88 BC.
21 April, the festival of Pales, pastoral goddess; the day Romans celebrated the founding of the city.
Festival created at Verres's command in the cities of Sicily, replacing the Marcellia at Syracuse and propagated through other towns; the contracts for its rites were let out years in advance.
Trojan hero, mythological founder of Segesta and ancestor of the Romans.
Greco-Roman god of healing; his shrine at Agrigentum held the Apollo by Myron, taken by Verres.
Olympian god of prophecy, music, and healing; born on Delos. The Delian temple was the most ancient and revered shrine of Apollo in the Greek world.

Roman goddess of grain, equated with Greek Demeter. Her cult at Henna was the most ancient and most reverenced in the Greek world; Verres carried off her statue, which Cicero treats as a second rape of Proserpina.
River-god of the Chrysas river near Assorus; his shrine held a marble statue Verres tried by night to plunder.
Olympian goddess of the hunt, equated with Greek Artemis; born on Delos with her twin Apollo. Her shrines at Perga and Ephesus were major Asian centres.
Roman god of the underworld, equated with Greek Hades; carried off Proserpina from Henna in the Sicilian myth.
Greek mythological queen, betrayed her husband Amphiaraus for the gold-and-gems necklace of Harmonia. Cicero invokes the type-figure of greed inflamed by sight.
Greco-Roman hero-god; his temple at Agrigentum held the famous bronze cult-statue (with worn lips and chin) that Verres tried to plunder by night.
The Punic-Greek-Roman goddess (Tanit/Astarte/Hera) of the great Maltese sanctuary on the promontory; her treasure stripped by Verres in three years of weaving and plunder.
The cult of Hera at the Heraion of Samos, one of the largest and most ancient sanctuaries in the Greek world. Verres looted statues from it during his Asian legateship.
Chief god of the Roman state, with his temple on the Capitol; addressed by consul Cicero in the Catilinarians.
Mother of Apollo and Diana, equated with Greek Leto; gave birth to the twins on Delos after long persecution by Hera.
Daughter of Ceres, equated with Greek Persephone; her rape from the grove of Henna by Father Dis is the Sicilian myth Cicero invokes for Verres's plundering of the cult statue.

The cult statue of Mercury given to the Tyndaritans by P. Scipio Africanus after the destruction of Carthage; carried off by Verres, the proagorus Sopater bound naked to Marcellus's equestrian statue in protest.
Olympian goddess of wisdom and craft, equated with Greek Athena; her temple on the Athenian acropolis is the Parthenon.
Eponymous founder-hero of Tenedos, worshipped there as a god; his cult statue, looted by Verres, was familiar to Romans because Cicero says it had once stood in the Comitium.
Hero of the Eleusinian and Sicilian mysteries; spread the cult of grain. His statue with Ceres stood before the Henna temple.
The Muse of astronomy; Cicero's chosen interlocutor in book 2 of De Consulatu Suo and the autobiographical poem De Temporibus Suis.
Venus of Eryx, the great Sicilian sanctuary on Mt. Eryx in the west of the island; her temple held vast treasure and slave-priestesses, and was a frequent pretext for Verres's confiscations.
Goddess of the hearth; her cult was tended by the Vestal Virgins at the temple in the Forum.
Roman personification of victory; the great image of Victory in Ceres's right hand at Henna was torn off by Verres.
Roman god of fire and the forge; his temple in the Forum Romanum was a venue for popular assemblies and political display.
The Roman state treasury, housed in the temple of Saturn; under quaestorian custody.
Roman court of (originally) 105 men chosen by tribe, hearing inheritance and property cases. The setting of several of De Oratore's reference cases (M.' Curius and Coponius, etc.).
Chief Roman priestly college, headed by the pontifex maximus; jurisdiction over sacral law, including consecration of property.
The Minucian clan, taken collectively as heir-by-default at law where a Minucius died intestate; Verres's edict overrode this customary succession.
The sovereign assembled body of citizens; legislator and elector, addressed by Cicero from the Rostra as Quirites.
The deliberative body of the Roman state, composed of ex-magistrates; addressed by Cicero as patres conscripti. Issued senatus consulta which had moral but not legal force.
Civil action for damage done by armed force, instituted by M. Lucullus as praetor in 76 BC; the form of action in Pro Tullio.
The annual edict in which each praetor laid out the formulae and remedies he would grant in his year of office; the framework of Roman civil procedure.
Roman civil law as held and interpreted by the jurisconsults — the body of legal practice within which inheritance, contracts, kinship, and property were governed.

Sullan statutes of 81 BC reforming the standing courts: lex Cornelia testamentaria de falsis (forgery and adulteration of wills) and lex Cornelia nummaria (counterfeit). Drafted prospectively in their criminal provisions.

Three older statutes (lex Atinia on usucapion of stolen things, lex Furia on testamentary legacies, lex Fusia/Furia Caninia or Fusia/Furia on manumissions) cited by Cicero as paragons of prospective legislation; not Verres's retroactive edict.

Two laws of c. 150 BC governing obnuntiatio (religious obstruction of legislation by claim of unfavourable omens). A safeguard of the senatorial class against popular tribunes; abolished by Clodius's tribunate in 58 BC.
Sullan statute of 81 BC governing the proscriptions; among other things forbade aiding the proscribed.

Sullan statute of 81 BC stripping the tribunes of the plebs of major political powers (legislation, prosecution, debarment from further office); the law against which Q. Opimius as tribune had vetoed and was prosecuted.
Rome's first written code of law (450 BC); memorised by Roman schoolboys into the late Republic. Crassus's hyperbole at De Or. 1.195: it surpasses the libraries of all the philosophers.
Sicilian grain-tithe law inherited by Rome from the kingdom of Hiero II of Syracuse; framework for the tithe-farming Verres allegedly broke; central to In Verrem II.3.
Caesar's law of 59 BC reorganising the regulation of provincial governors; widely held the most carefully framed of Roman extortion laws and the basis for later trials.
Pompey's law of 55 BC reforming the album of jurors by tribe and rank; in Cicero's hope, less liable to political packing than its predecessors.

Roman provincial charter for Sicily issued by P. Rupilius (132 BC) on the advice of the ten senatorial legates; specified the rules for jurisdiction between Sicilians of different cities, between Sicilians and Romans, and between communities and individuals. Verres systematically circumvented it.
Gaius Gracchus's law of 123 BC making the tax-farming of Asia let by the censors at Rome, the engine of publican-class wealth in the late Republic.

Grain-buying law of 73 BC by the consuls M. Terentius Varro Lucullus and C. Cassius Longinus, governing the senate's annual purchases of Sicilian grain in addition to the tithes; Cicero's standing reference for the legal frame Verres violated.

Lex Voconia (169 BC, tr. pl. Q. Voconius Saxa): forbade citizens of the highest census class from instituting women as heirs. Drafted prospectively, against which Verres's retroactive edict is contrasted.

aliases: senatus consultum de re publica defendenda
entities.summary: Senatorial decree commanding the consuls to see that the commonwealth take no harm; first issued in 121 BC against C. Gracchus, then in 100 BC against Saturninus, then in 63 BC against Catiline. Authorised use of force; its constitutional standing was challenged repeatedly by populares.
Junior magistracy concerned with the city, the markets, and the public games; a stepping-stone to the praetorship.
Two senior magistrates elected normally every five years to revise the lists of citizens, knights, and senators, and to let the public contracts; the highest office an ex-consul could hold.
Head of the Roman state; two were elected annually, gave their names to the year, commanded armies, and presided over the Senate.
Annually-elected magistrate ranking below consul; presided over the standing courts and (as praetor urbanus) issued the annual edict that governed civil procedure.
Legally-authorised agent or attorney for an absent principal; central to Pro Quinctio, where Sex. Alfenus stands in for the absent Quinctius.
Most junior of the regular magistracies; financial officers attached to the consuls and provincial governors.
Magistrate of the plebs (ten elected annually); could veto magistrates and senatorial decrees and convoke the plebeian assembly.
Tutor or agent who plundered the orphan of Drepanum under Verres; Metellus ordered him to restore the goods.
Roman tribune 67 (carried the lex Gabinia for Pompey against the pirates), consul 58 BC with L. Piso; governor of Syria 57-55 BC. Convicted on his return.

entities.canonical_name: Aulus Postumius Albinus and Quintus Fulvius Flaccus
entities.summary: Censors of 174 BC, named in Verres's edict on women's inheritances as the temporal anchor for retroactive rating; the chronology was tailored to the Annian case.
Brother of P. Trebonius, on the Sullan proscription lists; Verres barred his freedman from the inheritance because the freedman had sworn to help him in defiance of the lex Cornelia.
Verres's interpreter in Sicily, a worthless and needy man whom Verres made tithe-collector of the Liparenses.
Transalpine Gaul, guest-friend of Cn. Domitius Ahenobarbus; the wronged party in Domitius's prosecution of M. (or D.) Silanus c. 109 BC.

Halaesine of the highest talent, prudence, and authority; explained to Cicero in the Halaesine senate that Verres pocketed the requisitioned-grain payments and made the cities supply the grain in money instead.

Athenian orator (c. 389-314 BC), opponent of Demosthenes in the Crown trial; after his exile to Rhodes, allegedly delivered Demosthenes's reply with the famous concession that 'you would have wondered more if you had heard the man himself'.
Syracusan accomplice of Verres; one of those whose wife was a favourite, and an active manager of the gymnasiarch swindle against Heraclius.
Tyndaritan whom Verres robbed of a bowl.
The leading tragic actor of Cicero's day, friend of Cicero. By the games of 55 BC he was elderly; in Fam. 7.1 his voice fails him at the line ``si sciens fallo''.
Noble of Thermae, enemy of Sthenius; father-in-law of Dorotheus, whose wife Callidama was the new object of Verres's lust. Joined the prosecution of Sthenius.
Tyrant and king of Syracuse (r. 317-289 BC); the cavalry-battle panels in the Syracusan Minerva temple commemorated his battles.
Macedonian king (r. 336-323 BC); his portrait by Apelles at Ephesus was the famous example of the Greek city's painted treasures.
Pre-Socratic philosopher (c. 500-428 BC); the first to bring philosophy to Athens; teacher of Pericles.
Centuripine envoy to the Verres trial, with Artemo.
Senator and friend of Cicero, owner of an estate near Cicero's Pompeianum that could host visitors when the villa was unfinished.
Roman matron, unrated in the census, who made her daughter heir; cited by Cicero as proof that no praetor after Verres re-issued the retroactive edict.
Daughter and sole heir of P. Annius Asellus by his will; her ward-status is the reference-case in 2-2.21 of Verres's retroactive edict on women's inheritances.
Seleucid king (r. 222-187 BC); defeated by L. Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus at Magnesia 190 BC, ending Seleucid power in Asia Minor.

Last Seleucid king of Syria, son of Antiochus X Eusebes and Cleopatra Selene; he and his brother passed through Sicily on their way home in c. 75 BC and stopped at Syracuse, where Verres extorted from him the candelabrum he was bringing as a Capitoline gift.
Consul 44 BC, triumvir 43 BC; target of the fourteen Philippics; Cicero's death warrant came from him after the proscriptions of November 43 BC.
Sicilian kinsman of Dio of Halaesa; from him a great inheritance fell to Dio's son, the pretext for Verres's extortion under the Erucine Venus claim.
Chief man of Agyrium; gave the public testimony at the actio prima against Verres.
Drepanitan made Roman citizen, called Aulus Clodius; Verres helped him plunder the orphans of Drepanum, then took silver from him.
Elder brother of Clodius; consul 54 BC, censor 50 BC, governor of Cilicia 53--51 BC (Cicero's predecessor there); recipient of Fam. book 3.
Verres's bagman in the Sicilian grain extortions; the principal villain of In Verrem II.3.
Praetor 66 BC and noted jurist; contemporary of Cicero, mentioned in Att. 4.12 in the Halimetus matter.
Noble of Haluntium charged by Verres with collecting the embossed silver of the town --- a public despoiling carried out by the man who most longed to be loved by his fellow-citizens.
Greek poet of Antioch; client of the Lucullus family; defendant in Pro Archia (62 BC) in a citizenship-status case. Cicero's defence is the famous panegyric of literary culture.
Syracusan mathematician and engineer (c. 287-212 BC), killed at the sack of Syracuse despite Marcellus's order to spare him.
Most noble Helorian; testified before Cicero that the tithe-collector had declared more was owed than he could raise from all his goods.
The Greek philosopher (384--322 BC); a presence in Cicero's library and study, image (imago) of whom hung above Atticus's reading-chair.
Panhormite from whom Verres took noble trappings.
Lampsacene against whom Verres gave testimony before Nero in the case of the riot, accusing him of stirring up the people.

Greek doctor from Perga in Pamphylia; once Verres's leader in plundering the temple of Diana at Perga, then his retainer in Sicily under the Roman name Cornelius. Standing recoverer for Apronius's tithe cases.
Centuripine envoy to the Verres trial; in the actio prima Hortensius said his energy made him "a prosecutor, not a witness."
Cephaloeditan who paid Verres for the priesthood of his town; Verres dropped 45 days from the calendar so that the elections fell before his rival Herodotus could return from Rome.
Leading men of Entella, envoys to the Verres trial with Sositheus.
Greek physician (c. 124-40 BC), settled at Rome; the dominant medical authority of the late Republic. Friend of Crassus the orator.
Cilician slave-leader of the Second Sicilian Servile War (104-100 BC); Cicero contrasts the limited reach of his rebellion with the universal reach of Timarchides's tyranny.
Verres's minister in tithe matters; bought the Herbitan tithes in the first year and extorted double the value as gain from the city.
Young prosecutor in Pro Caelio (56 BC); son of L. Calpurnius Bestia, whom Caelius had himself prosecuted earlier in 56 BC.
Wealthy Netine, weaving for Verres.
Cicero's lifelong intimate (110–32 BC), wealthy Epicurean banker, recipient of the Ad Atticum letters; later Q. Cicero's brother-in-law.
Slave of Erucine Venus made tithe-collector at Amestratus; received 850 medimni and 1,500 sesterces from the Amestratines.
Tribune; prosecuted by Caelius earlier in 56 BC; father of the prosecutor Atratinus in Pro Caelio.
Greek sculptor and silversmith of the 2nd century BC; his hydria taken by Verres from Pamphilus.
Tetrarch of the Trocmi (Galatia) and son-in-law of Deiotarus; bought the priesthood of Cybele at Pessinus in 58 BC under a Clodian law.
The tyrannicide (85–42 BC); praetor 44 BC; one of the principal assassins of Caesar; recipient of the Ad Brutum letters; defeated and dead at Philippi.
Senatorial juror notorious for venality; named with Staienus by Cicero as the type of corrupt jury-buyer Verres outdoes. Convicted of taking bribes.
Praetor at Rome at the time Naevius applied to attach Quinctius's goods; his order to advertise the sale is the procedural anchor of the speech.
Roman senator forced to pay the Venusian slave Symmachus money for tithes besides the grain.
Roman consul 63 BC as Cicero's colleague; uncle of Mark Antony. Cicero exchanged provinces with him so as to keep him quiet during the Catilinarian crisis.
The praetor and jurist (d. c. 44 BC) presiding as iudex in Pro Quinctio; a celebrated authority on civil law, author of the actio de dolo malo.
Brother of L. Aurelius; commended together with him to Q. Ancharius in Fam. 13.40.
Busy and resourceful Roman whose furniture Verres took.
Judge addressed by Cicero in Pro Roscio Comoedo; consul 67 BC.
Roman consul 73 BC with M. Terentius Varro Lucullus; defeated by Spartacus the same year.
Consul 114 BC; convicted of extortion and fined 8,000 sesterces, the proverbial small-fine that Cicero contrasts with the 1,300,000 sesterces Verres's clerk skimmed.
Consul 49 BC alongside Lentulus Crus; one of three Marcelli of the Caesarian-civil-war years; cousin of M. Marcellus, consul 51 BC.
Praetor 81 BC, governor of Asia 79 BC; before him Philodamus and his son were tried and condemned, with Dolabella exerting pressure on Verres's behalf.
Roman of the Palatine tribe; Verres's go-between in Sicily, charged in the records as Sthenius's representative against Sthenius's actual will.
Praetor 95 BC, consul 92 BC; gave the Halaesians their law on the co-optation of the senate, by senatorial decree, advised by the Marcelli.
Roman orator (124-73 BC), consul 75; an interlocutor in De Oratore as a young man. Cicero's source for the conversation: De Oratore is presented as Cotta's recollection.
Roman consul 75 BC, the orator of De Oratore. Burned with desire for a triumph but was prevented by death.
Roman consul 76 BC, governor of Macedonia, triumphed in 72 BC over the Dardanians. Father of the tribune of 50 BC.
Prosecutor in Pro Roscio Comoedo; suing Roscius the actor over a partnership in the slave-actor Panurgus.
Roman commander in Gaul at the time of the events of Pro Quinctio (83 BC); Quinctius took refuge with him after being driven from the partnership land.
Roman citizen against whom Verres ordered (irregularly) summary execution-by-arrest under his Sicilian edict.
Senator who at the new praetor Metellus's coming asked for an action against Apronius under the Octavian formula "for what he had carried off by force or fear." Metellus refused.
Roman tribune of the plebs 123-122 BC, brother of Tiberius Gracchus; the second great popularis orator; killed in 121 BC. The famous Whither-shall-I-flee passage (De Or. 3.214) survives only here.
Roman orator and tragic poet (c. 130-87 BC), aedile 90, half-brother of Catulus. Speaker in De Oratore Book 2 on humour. Killed in the Marian-Sullan strife.

Roman quaesitor (presiding judge) at the trial of Oppianicus 74 BC; convicted in the public outrage that followed, after Verres allegedly forged the album of jurors. The same scandal is the spine of Pro Cluentio.

Roman consul 140 BC, intimate friend of Scipio Aemilianus; the byword for moral seriousness in late-republican memory. Cicero pairs him with Cato as the standard against which a young man's character is measured.
Quaestor to Cn. Dolabella in Cilicia, killed there 80 BC; Verres succeeded him as deputy quaestor and seized his goods, then plundered his orphan ward.
Roman consul 137 BC; surrendered to the Numantines after defeat, was handed over to them by senatorial decree, but the surrender was rejected and his civil status was contested.
Praetor 80 BC, governor of Sicily 79 BC after the disorders of M. Aemilius Lepidus's commands; restored order. Father of the Marcellus who was consul 50 BC. Distinct from C. Marcellus cos 49 BC.
Roman general and statesman (157-86 BC), seven-time consul; victor over Jugurtha and over the Cimbri and Teutones. The dominant political figure of Cicero's youth.
Roman knight kept under guard by Apronius for two days at Leontini, denied food and shelter, until he agreed to Apronius's tithe terms.
Roman knight and publican, stepfather of the ward Iunius; one of the family who appealed in vain first to Verres and then to Chelidon.
Tribune of the plebs c. 103 BC; prosecuted Caepio and was himself prosecuted under the lex Appuleia de maiestate; defended (and acquitted) by Antonius the orator.
Marian consul 83 BC with L. Cornelius Scipio Asiagenus; suicide 82 BC at Rhodes after the Sullan victory. Earlier as praetor of Sicily he had sold the tithes by the Hieronic law without complaint.

Tribune 130 BC, consul 120 BC; turncoat from popularis to optimate. Prosecuted in 119 BC by L. Crassus the orator (then a young man) and convicted; took his own life. Cicero cites Crassus's regret at having taken up the prosecution.
Cicero's first son-in-law, husband of Tullia (married c. 63 BC); he died young in 57 BC, much mourned by Cicero. Of the Calpurnian gens but a different branch from L. Piso Caesoninus.
Brother of Publius Quinctius; partner with Sextus Naevius in a Gallic cattle and farming business; died in Gaul before the events of the speech.
Praetor 75 BC, governor of Sicily 74 BC immediately preceding Verres; left the province in good order, by contrast.
Praetor 94 BC, governor of Macedonia c. 93-87 BC; cited by Cicero as a man of the old singular innocence who took a great sum from his rations during a grain-dearth without scandal.
Senator notorious for corrupt jury-buying, in 74 BC the broker of bribes in the Oppianicus trial; he is the central figure in the Pro Cluentio narrative.
Roman testator who died under Sacerdos's praetorship; Verres later edicted to grant his patron Sulpicius's daughter a sixth, against M. Octavius Ligus the heir.
Military tribune in Asia 79 BC; testified that Philodamus had told him directly of Verres's outrage at Lampsacum.

The fictitious name written over the erasures of "Verres" in Carpinatius's account-books at Syracuse; the mark of Verres's attempt to launder the receipts. "Verrine tail, as if dipped in mud, sunk into an erasure" (2-2.191).
Roman noblewoman, daughter of Q. Caecilius Metellus Balearicus; sheltered Sex. Roscius the younger after his father's murder.
Defendant in Pro Caecina (69 BC); Etruscan landowner whose interdictum de vi armata claim against Aebutius is the form of action.
Younger orator and politician; Cicero's protege; defendant in Pro Caelio (4 April 56 BC), the famous Clodia speech; later a correspondent in the Ad Familiares.
The conqueror of Gaul, dictator, and adversary of the Republic (100–44 BC); consul 59 BC; Cicero's patron in 56 BC after Luca; assassinated on the Ides of March.
Daughter of Agathinus, wife of Dorotheus; the new object of Verres's lust at Thermae.
Greek philosopher (214-129 BC), head of the Academy at Athens; famous for arguing on either side, including the embassy of 155 BC to Rome with Diogenes and Critolaus.
The patrician conspirator (c. 108–62 BC) whose plot against the consular government in 63 BC Cicero exposed and crushed; killed at the battle of Pistoria in January 62 BC.
Roman statesman (234-149 BC), consul 195, censor 184; author of the Origines and De Agri Cultura. The model of the orator-jurist-soldier-citizen for Cicero.
The Stoic statesman (95–46 BC), great-grandson of the Censor; tribune 62 BC; champion of senatorial constitutionalism; suicide at Utica after Pharsalus.
Catilinarian conspirator; possessor of the cache of swords in his house; executed with Lentulus 5 December 63 BC.
Trierarch of Chios; testified at the actio prima that as Verres's escort home from Asia he saw the Samian Heraion plundered, and was himself acquitted on related charges before his fellow Chians.
Greek philosopher of the New Academy (c. 165-95 BC); pupil of Carneades. Crassus heard him at Athens in 110 BC; Cicero presents him as the foil to the rhetoricians.
Verres's mistress during his city praetorship 74 BC; her house was the de facto seat of judgement, where edicts were brokered for cash. The name ("Swallow") was a courtesan's stage-name.
Sulla's most influential freedman, in charge of the proscription auctions; the unnamed power behind the prosecution in Pro Roscio Amerino.
A slave of Verres named in his accounts as the recipient of 600,000 sesterces from the embezzlement of Malleolus's estate. Distinct from Sulla's freedman L. Cornelius Chrysogonus of Pro Roscio.
The orator, statesman, and philosopher (106–43 BC); author of the corpus. Consul 63 BC.
Syracusan put in command of the Sicilian fleet by Verres in 71 BC; his abandonment of the fleet at Helorus and the resulting loss are the spine of In Verrem II.5.
Sister of P. Clodius and widow of Q. Metellus Celer; the (probable) Lesbia of Catullus; the unnamed accuser behind the prosecution in Pro Caelio.
The popularis tribune (c. 93–52 BC) whose 58 BC bill drove Cicero into exile; a patrician adopted into a plebeian family to qualify for the tribunate; killed by Milo's gang on the Appian Way.
Defendant in Pro Cluentio (66 BC); accused of poisoning his stepfather Oppianicus; Cicero's defence is the longest of the forensic speeches and turns on the eight-year shadow of the Junian trial.
Splendid Roman knight, businessman in Sicily; from him Verres took silver horsemen attributed to Q. Fabius Maximus. His son was a senator and juror.
Roman consul 81 BC, governor of Macedonia, triumphed; convicted at Cicero's prosecution in 77.
The praetor (NOT P. Cornelius Dolabella the consul) before whom Naevius later sought to compel the sponsio that became the form of action in Pro Quinctio.

Roman consul 96 BC, censor 92 BC; in his youth prosecuted M. (or D.) Iunius Silanus for wrongs done to his transalpine guest-friend Aegritomarus, the precedent Cicero invokes for taking up Sthenius's case.

entities.canonical_name: Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus and Lucius Caecilius Metellus (censors)
entities.summary: Censors of 115 BC, cited by Cicero as paragons of legitimate edict-making, in contrast to Verres.
Roman knight, brother of Q. Titinius the juror; testified that he had paid Verres money and produced his account-books.
Roman consul 72 BC, censor 70 BC; with L. Gellius brought the case of Sthenius before the senate and supported the principle that no man should be made defendant in his absence in the provinces.
Roman senator (cos 56 BC); dissuaded Archagathus from demanding his money back at Rome, which became a piece of the testimony at the Verres trial.
Marian leader, three times consul (85, 84, 82 BC); killed in Sicily by Pompey 82 BC. Verres was his quaestor in Cisalpine Gaul 84 BC and deserted him with the public money.
Sicilian Greek made Roman citizen by Pompey, called Tullius among his fellow citizens; provided lodging at Messana for Cicero on his Sicilian inquiry.
Tyndaritan made Roman citizen by Pompey, hence renamed Cn. Pompeius; victim of Verres's tearing-off of figures from his banquet-dish.
Sicilian made Roman citizen by Pompey; witness at Verres's trial.
Tithe-collector at Hybla; carried off six times the sown amount from the farmers, on Cicero's reckoning.
Roman knight, witness at the Verres trial.
Verres's lictor at Lampsacum; killed in the riot during the attempt on Philodamus's daughter.
Verres's scriba; received money skimmed from the temple-of-Castor contract.
King of the Sapaean Thracians, a Roman ally; bought from Piso the head of Rabocentus, chief of the rival Bessian people, for 300 talents.
Cicero's son-in-law (briefly): married Tullia in early 56 BC after her widowhood; the marriage did not last. The horti Crassipedis are Cicero's favourite suburban dining spot in 55 BC.
The wealthiest Roman of his age (115–53 BC); consul 70, 55 BC; suppressor of Spartacus; member of the First Triumvirate; killed at Carrhae.
Tyndaritan from whom Verres took noble trappings.
Aetnan, weaving for Verres.
Roman whose lands stood as security for L. Habonius's contract on the temple of Castor; pressed Verres to remit part of the payment.
A courier or freedman of Atticus's mentioned in Att. 4.11 as the man through whom certain matters can be settled; not certainly identifiable with Demetrius of Magnesia.
Gymnasiarch of Tyndaris in charge of the place where the Mercury stood; oversaw its removal at Verres's order.
Greek polymath, biographer and bibliographer of Cicero's day; cited in the letters as a literary correspondent.
Athenian orator and statesman (384-322 BC); the supreme Greek orator of antiquity in Cicero's view. The Philippics and the De Corona are his canonical works.
Head of the Egyptian embassy of 57 BC opposing Ptolemy XII Auletes; murdered in Rome under suspicious circumstances. Caelius was charged with complicity in the murder.
Sicilian notable from Halaesa; Verres summoned him under pretext that an inheritance fallen to his son had passed to Erucine Venus, and extorted a million sesterces from him.
Well-off Centuripine who hanged himself the day Apronius bought the tithes of his field.
Illustrious Panhormitan farming a leased estate in Segestan territory; beaten by the Venusian Symmachus into paying 16,000 sesterces and 654 medimni for tithes.
Son-in-law of Pamphilus of Lilybaeum, surnamed Popilius; from him Verres carried off all the silver of the sideboard.
Resident of Lilybaeum, owner of cups by Mentor; absented himself from the province to keep his silver. Verres set up a fictive capital charge against him in absentia.
Senior Syracusan senator; spokesman in the senate when Cicero was invited to address it on Verres's plunder.
Public slave of Erucine Venus made tithe-collector by Verres; harassed the Tissans for the wheat-tithe.
Highest magistrate of Catina, charged by Verres with seeking out and surrendering all the silver in the city.
Syracusan accomplice of Verres in the Heraclius extortion and the looting of the Syracusan public buildings.
Tertia's protector to whom Verres handed the barley tithes of Herbita and the tithes of Acesta. With Aeschrio one of the foul little replacements for legitimate tithe-collectors.
Caesarian, consul 44 BC, married to Tullia 50 BC. Charming and impossible; a constant source of correspondence-borne anxiety to Cicero.
Husband of Callidama; Verres moved into his house at Thermae after breaking with Sthenius.
Roman knight and friend of Atticus and Cicero, often acting as a financial agent; appears in Att. 4.12 and the later Atticus correspondence.
Roman epic poet (239--169 BC); author of the Annales (the first Roman national epic in hexameters); revered by Cicero as the great early Latin poet, frequently quoted in the corpus.
Greek historian (c. 400-c. 330 BC), pupil of Isocrates; author of the lost universal history in 30 books.

Leading citizen of Bidis; an inheritance of 500,000 sesterces fell to him by law, and Verres stripped him on a fictive charge of corrupting public records, then added 1,500,000 sesterces of his ancestral property.
Prosecutor in Pro Roscio Amerino; Cicero treats him with deliberate contempt as a hireling of the Roscii.
Centuripine of leading wealth and noble birth; one of Apronius's particular victims, beaten by him.
Calactine, guest-friend of the Luculli; Verres tore the insets from his banquet cups in his presence.
Son of the dictator Sulla (born ca. 86 BC); married Pompey's daughter; served as quaestor in Syria with Pompey; killed in Africa after Thapsus 46 BC. Owner of a notable library at Cumae.
Praetor 63 BC, propraetor of Asia 62 BC; defendant in Pro Flacco (mid-59 BC) on extortion charges; one of Cicero's allies during the Catilinarian crisis.
Former praetorian governor of Transalpine Gaul (76–73 BC); defendant in Pro Fonteio (69 BC), prosecuted for extortion by an embassy of Gauls under Indutiomarus.

Tribune 67 BC (proposer of the lex Gabinia for Pompey's pirate command), consul 58 BC; one of the consuls under whom Cicero went into exile, hence one of the "two whirlwinds of the commonwealth" of Pro Sestio.
Tribune 56 BC, defended by Cicero on a maiestas charge in 55 BC; the trial in which Cicero ``almost killed himself'' (dirupi me paene) according to Fam. 7.1.
Greek sophist and rhetorician (c. 483-376 BC); reputedly the first to take any topic at the audience's request. The figure of Plato's Gorgias.
Generic reference to one of the famous tribunes Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus; here invoked as the kind of revolutionary name whose presence might stir popular sympathy.
Carthaginian general (247-c. 183 BC), commander in the Second Punic War. The figure of the Hannibal/Phormio anecdote in De Oratore 2.75-76.
Generic Carthaginian commander invoked as an enemy in Sicily during the Punic Wars.
Wealthy and respected citizen of Messana; his shrine of Eros, a small Cupid by Praxiteles, and a Hercules by Myron were taken by Verres in 73 BC; the principal opening victim of In Verrem II.4.
Amestratine envoy compelled to count out 22,000 sesterces.
Centuripine youth, witness at Verres's trial; Verres extorted 400,000 sesterces from him and afterwards declared the favourable judgement on a compromise void.
Wealthiest Syracusan of his time; on the inheritance of three million sesterces from his kinsman Heraclius, Verres rigged the gymnasiarch suit against him and stripped his ancestral fortune.
Roman businessman from the African province; recognized at Syracuse by more than a hundred Romans, but executed by Verres's order on a charge of being a Sertorian.
Greek historian of Halicarnassus (c. 484-425 BC), author of the Histories; the canonical first historian.
Cephaloeditan who would have been priest had he been present at the elections; Verres's calendar reform kept him out. Distinct from the Greek historian.
Wax-modeller from Cibyra, brother of Tlepolemus; one of Verres's two chief Greek agents in the systematic plundering of Sicilian art.
King of Syracuse (270–215 BC); a steady ally of Rome through the Punic Wars; framer of the lex Hieronica that governed Sicilian grain taxation under the Roman province.
King of Syracuse (r. 270-215 BC); Roman ally throughout the First and (until his death) Second Punic Wars. Author of the lex Hieronica, the grain-tithe charter Rome kept on annexing Sicily.
Greek sophist (c. 460-400 BC), boasted of his polymath self-sufficiency: he had made the very ring, cloak, and sandals he wore (De Or. 3.127).
The Greek poet of the Iliad and Odyssey; the literary touchstone for Cicero, especially in the letters where Greek tags from Homer appear constantly.
The leading orator of his generation before Cicero (114–50 BC); consul 69 BC. The opposing counsel in Pro Quinctio and the prosecutor in In Verrem; later a friend.
Athenian rhetorician (436-338 BC), founder of the great Athenian school of rhetoric; teacher of Theopompus, Ephorus, and many leading Greek orators.
King of Numidia; ally of the Pompeians in the African campaign of 47--46 BC; defeated at Thapsus, took his own life.
Deformed Roman, mocked by Crassus on the bench (De Or. 2.262); the figure of the spirited self-defence with quick wit.
Roman consul 168 BC; defeated King Perseus of Macedon at Pydna, ending the Antigonid kingdom. Father of Scipio Aemilianus.
Pompeian legate of long standing; consul 60 BC; commander of two of Pompey's Spanish legions in 49 BC; killed in Africa in 46 BC after Thapsus.
Quinctius's travel companion to Gaul on 29 January 83 BC, named in the speech as a respectable witness to the date of departure.
Substitute heir named in P. Annius Asellus's will after the daughter; bought from Verres the retroactive edict that gave him the inheritance.
Young man, son of Lucius; one of the two Aurelii commended by Cicero to the proconsul Q. Ancharius in Fam. 13.40.
Praetor 71 BC, governor of Sicily 70-69 BC, immediately succeeding Verres; cleared the residue of Verres's plunder. Consul 68 BC.
Witness at the Verres trial on the Dio extortion; otherwise unknown.
Witness at the Verres trial on the Dio extortion.

entities.canonical_name: Lucius Calpurnius Piso Frugi (cos 133, tr. pl. 149)
entities.summary: Roman tribune 149 BC, consul 133 BC; brought the lex Calpurnia, the first standing law on extortion (de repetundis), in 149 BC. The byword for an honest provincial governor.
Praetor 74 BC, colleague of Verres in the city praetorship; an honest jurist whose tribunal was a refuge from Verres's corrupt court. Father of Cicero's son-in-law C. Piso Frugi.

Roman praetor in Hispania, killed there. Father of L. Piso the praetor 74 BC. Cicero cites his Corduba-forum gold-weighing as the proverbial old-school transparency, contrasted with Verres's gold-vase workshop at Syracuse.

Manager of the harbour-customs station at Syracuse for the publicani; his letters to the partners listed the goods Verres exported without harbour duty. Cicero seized two of his ledgers when investigating in Sicily.

Assistant manager of the publicani's scriptura partnership in Sicily; bound himself to Verres in close partnership and money-lending; his books, with their erasures of Verres's name and substitutions of "Verrucius," became the most damning material evidence at the trial.

entities.canonical_name: Lucius Cassius Longinus and Gnaeus Servilius Caepio (censors)
entities.summary: Censors of 125 BC, cited as paragons of legitimate edict-making.
Marian consul 83 BC with C. Norbanus; defeated by Sulla and proscribed.
Roman consul 190 BC, brother of Scipio Africanus; conqueror of Antiochus III at Magnesia. (Distinct from L. Scipio Asiagenus consul 83 BC.)
Roman historian, praetor 78 BC; one of Verres's defenders. Author of a history of the Sullan civil wars in twelve or more books.

entities.canonical_name: Lucius Licinius Crassus and Quintus Mucius Scaevola (consuls 95 BC)
entities.summary: The two great senators of the late 90s, consuls together 95 BC; their year is the date of the Halaesian senatorial law given by C. Claudius Pulcher with the Marcelli's advice.
Roman to whom Verres returned silver through Potamo, before stopping such returns.
Senator, Cicero's witness at the actio prima; consul 54 BC, killed at Pharsalus 48 BC. Son-in-law of Cato.
Roman renegade resident at Myndus, named with L. Magius; Senate-declared enemy of Rome who bought Verres's stolen Milesian ship.
Roman knight, witness at the Verres trial; testified that his friend Herennius, a businessman from Africa, was struck with the axe by Verres at Syracuse.
Roman consul 72 BC, censor 70 BC; one of the senatorial elders of Cicero's day. Spoke in support of Cicero in 63 BC, declaring he had earned a civic crown.
Guardian of the ward Iunius and chosen contractor in Verres's rigged award; received 560,000 sesterces to whitewash four columns of the temple of Castor.
Praetor c. 81 BC, Q. Hortensius's father; sold the Sicilian tithes by the Hieronic law without complaint. Distinct from his more famous son.
Friend of P. Cuspius commended to Q. Valerius Orca in Fam. 13.6; otherwise unattested.

The supreme Roman orator of Cicero's youth (140-91 BC); consul 95, censor 92, princeps senatus. Speaker in De Oratore; his death of pleurisy ten days after the Ides of September 91 BC frames Book 3 as elegy. Not to be confused with M. Licinius Crassus the triumvir.
Roman propraetor in Asia 84-81 BC under whom the Milesians and other Asian cities had built ten ships against pirates from tax-money. Father of L. Licinius Murena consul 62 BC.
Witness at the Verres trial on the Dio extortion; probably a relative of M. Octavius Ligus.
Praetor 67 BC; historian; recipient of Fam. 5.12 (the famous letter in which Cicero asks him to write up the consulship monographically).
Consul 74 BC; commander in the Mithridatic War; brought Archias to Rome under his patronage; great-grandfather of the Lucullan luxury tradition.
Roman renegade resident at Myndus; voted by the Senate among the enemies of Rome. Bought from Verres the Milesian ship that should have served the Roman fleet.
One of Cicero's two clerks during his Sicilian quaestorship 75 BC; cited as exemplary in not skimming a penny.
Roman consul 65 BC; supporter of Cicero, advocate of his recall from exile. Triumphed from Macedonia.

entities.canonical_name: Lucius Marcius Philippus and Marcus Perperna (censors)
entities.summary: Censors of 86 BC, named in Verres's edict on the temple of Castor as the temporal anchor for the contract.
Roman consul 91 BC; opponent of Drusus's reform programme. The opponent of Crassus's last great speech (De Or. 3.4).
Roman consul 146 BC; sacked Corinth and reduced Achaia to a Roman province; renowned for plain manners despite vast spoils.
Roman jurist, taken by Cicero as a hypothetical model judge; killed in the Marian-Cinnan massacres 82 BC.
Roman consul 75 BC with C. Aurelius Cotta; let out the contracts for sacred buildings under which the temple of Castor case arose.
Roman consul 121 BC; carried the senatus consultum ultimum against Gaius Gracchus and killed him. Convicted by the Mamilian commission in 109 BC and exiled.
Wealthy and honourable Roman knight; testified that Verres returned a censer to him with the inset torn out.
Wealthy literary friend of Cicero's, resident at Naples; recipient of the long sequence Fam. 9.15--9.26 in the late 50s and 40s.
Roman senator, consul 58 BC with A. Gabinius; father-in-law of Julius Caesar (whose daughter Calpurnia he had given Caesar in marriage). Defendant of In Pisonem.
An associate of Naevius bringing slaves for sale from Gaul; sighted Quinctius at Vada Volaterrana and reported it to Naevius, triggering the procedural ambush.
Counsel for the defence in Pro Tullio; tribune 74 BC, an opponent of senatorial control of the courts.
Roman citizen ordered to be led to court by Verres's edict.

Roman who challenged Apronius to a sponsion at Syracuse: "Unless you are saying that Verres is your partner in the tithes..." Verres quietly suppressed the suit. (Distinct from the procurer Rubrius of the Lampsacum affair.)
One of Cicero's two clerks during his Sicilian quaestorship.
Roman witness at the Verres trial; testified that many Roman citizens had been put to death by Verres's order in the Syracusan stone-quarries.
Roman citizen ordered to be led to court by Verres's edict. Possibly the same Lucius Suettius as the witness from the actio prima.
Roman citizen at Agrigentum whose ring Verres seized after noticing its seal in the wax of an intercepted letter.
Urban quaestor with P. Lentulus 84 BC; before him Verres rendered his quaestorian accounts in three lines.
Roman knight, then-recent master of the publicans' Sicilian scriptura partnership; the keeper of the office copy of the partnership letters at Rome.
Roman knight, recent master of the publicani's Sicilian scriptura partnership; from his copies Cicero recovered the suppressed records.
Pompey's praefectus fabrum and trusted confidant; the man with whom Cicero went late at night to Pompey's house in February 55 BC (Q.fr. 2.7).
Member of L. Metellus's staff in Sicily 70/69 BC; targeted by Timarchides's letter as the man through whom Apronius might soften the new praetor.
Crassus's mother-in-law (daughter of C. Laelius the wise); her preserved old Roman accent is praised by Crassus as 'Plautine' (De Or. 3.45).
Verres's regular courier between Rome and Sicily; brought the letter to L. Metellus that suddenly turned the new governor friendly.
Wealthy Segestan matron whose household made Verres purple-dyed coverlets for three years.
Consul 49 BC; staunch Pompeian; killed in Egypt in 48 BC after Pharsalus.
Consul 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; recipient of the long Egyptian-question correspondence in Ad Familiares book 1 (early 56 BC).
Praetor 63 BC, Catilinarian conspirator, executed in the Tullianum on Cicero's orders 5 December 63 BC.
Agent of Q. Caecilius Metellus Nepos at Rome, charged with the books of his Spanish province (Fam. 5.3).
Leading Lilybaean, Verres's host; the statue of Apollo plundered from his house was the marker of Verres's hospitality-violations.
The Lepidus whose Sicilian governorship preceded C. Marcellus's; identification disputed (perhaps the consul 78 BC, or another).
Son of the great M. Aemilius Scaurus; prosecuted Cn. Dolabella for extortion in 78 BC and turned Verres into a witness against his patron. Praetor 56 BC, defended by Cicero in 54.
Roman consul 115 BC, princeps senatus from 115 to 89 BC; the towering moral authority of the late second century, byword for senatorial gravity.
Roman knight resident at Syracuse; testified at the actio prima that he had seen a Roman citizen struck with the axe by Verres's order.
Praetor 74 BC, with extraordinary command against pirates over the whole Mediterranean; his oppressive levies on the Sicilian coast left a memory of mismanagement. Father of the triumvir Mark Antony.
Roman orator (143-87 BC), consul 99, censor 97; with Crassus the supreme orator of Cicero's youth. Grandfather of Mark Antony the triumvir. Speaker in De Oratore; killed by Marius's partisans in 87.
Roman consul 101 BC, victor in the Sicilian Slave War; defended in a repetundae trial by Antonius the orator (the famous tunic-tearing scene of De Or. 2.124, 195).
Consul 101 BC; suppressed the Second Sicilian Servile War (104-100 BC) under Athenion. Killed by Mithridates VI in 88 BC.
Roman quaestor who was forcibly prevented at Ephesus from removing his slave from the asylum of Diana; on his complaint Pericles of Ephesus was summoned to Rome.
Praetor 75 BC, charged with reviewing the patching of sacred buildings together with C. Sacerdos; the surviving items were referred to Verres and P. Caelius for decision.
Tithe-collector at Amestratus under Verres. Distinct from M. Caesius the praetor 75 BC.
Roman knight rewarded by Verres in Sicily.
Roman consul 195 BC, censor 184 BC; the moralist and historian whose praise of Sicily as cellam atque nutricem is quoted by Cicero. Great-grandfather of Cato Uticensis.
Roman consul 166, 155, 152 BC, victor over the Alpine Insubres; perished at sea on a later embassy. Cicero's example of an undeserved death.
Roman knight at Lilybaeum from whom Verres took what he wished.
Distinguished Roman, defender of Xeno of Menae before Verres.
Roman knight rewarded by Verres in Sicily; possibly the same as the M. Cossutius who defended Xeno of Menae.
Praetor 84 BC, then governor of Africa; burnt alive by Roman citizens at Utica 82 BC for his greed and cruelty.
Praetor 70 BC, presiding judge of the quaestio de repetundis at Verres's trial; later consul 67 BC.
Quinctius's former advocate, absent on a fresh embassy when Pro Quinctio was delivered; Cicero replaced him at short notice.

Roman accuser-orator (early first century BC), of base reputation; ridiculed by Crassus on the bench in the Plancius case. Not to be confused with Cicero's friend M. Iunius Brutus the assassin of Caesar.
Uncle and guardian of the ward Iunius, son of P. Iunius the contractor; testified at the actio prima about the temple of Castor extortion.
Tribune of the plebs 91 BC; carried (and lost) a sweeping reform programme — judicial, agrarian, and citizenship; assassinated late in 91 BC. His death precipitated the Social War.
Son of Q. Lollius the senior; testified at the trial in his ailing father's place.
Senator retained by Verres on the jury despite his patron's expectation; one of those Verres thought favourable to himself.
Roman consul 73 BC; brother of L. Lucullus; triumphed over the Bessi from Macedonia in 71 BC. Supporter of Cicero's recall.
Brother of L. Lucullus; praetor 76 BC, instituted the formula for damnum vi armata datum which is the form of action in Pro Tullio; consul 73 BC.
Marcellus's young descendant; Verres claimed the Tyndaritans had sold him the Mercury statue, hoping he would take the alleged purchase upon himself --- which he did not.

Distinguished guardian of the ward Iunius; appealed in person to Verres, whose authority alone could not move him. Probably the M. Marcellus who was curule aedile 56 BC; distinct from the conqueror of Syracuse.
Cicero's near neighbour and intimate friend at Cumae and Pompeii; recipient of Fam. 7.1--7.4 (the Cumae-circle letters), distinguished as a man of leisured cultivation.
Roman knight, witness at the Verres trial.
Roman urban praetor 70 BC, recipient of Metellus's letter from Sicily.
Roman senator instituted heir by Sulpicius Olympus; appealed in vain to Verres at Rome, who insisted he and his dogs needed the money.
Worthless Roman who allowed himself to be set up by Verres as Sthenius's prosecutor in absence; failed even to appear on the appointed day.
Tribune of the plebs 71 BC; Pompeian populist who agitated for the restoration of tribunician power and complained in public meeting about Sthenius's case.
Roman knight on Verres's consilium at the Sopater retrial; the device of his "private case" was Verres's pretext for dismissing the consilium that would have acquitted Sopater again.
Praetor 64 BC; Cicero's man on the Senate floor through the Egyptian commission of 56 BC; bearer of dispatches between Rome and Lentulus Spinther in Cilicia.
Greek freedman of Atticus's, scholar; tutor to the young M. Cicero and Q. Cicero; reading-companion of Cicero's at Cumae in 55 BC.
Quaestor under Verres in Sicily; allotted suits at the Syracuse conventus by the lex Rupilia, except the Heraclius case which Verres handled himself.
Quaestor to L. Cornelius Scipio Asiagenus the consul 83 BC; refused to take the money or join the army when he disagreed with the cause, contrasted by Cicero with Verres. Later consul 61 BC.
Roman consul 267 and 256 BC; captured by the Carthaginians and (in the legend) tortured to death after returning to Rome on parole and advising the senate against terms.
Common acquaintance of Naevius and Quinctius, named by Naevius as his arbiter in the partnership dispute.
Plaintiff in Pro Tullio (NOT the orator); his slaves and pasture were attacked by armed slaves of P. Fabius.
Roman consul 210 BC; commander in Sicily during the Second Punic War; reorganized the province after the recovery of Syracuse.
Young noble who took up Roscius's cause and brought it to court; later consul 61 BC.
Augur and consul 53 BC after long delay; author of works on the auspices and on Roman families; nephew of Hortensius.
Friend of Atticus, attending an auction at Larinum on the Ides of May 55 BC; otherwise unknown.
Cicero's son (65 BC – c. 25 BC); studied at Athens; a tribune under Brutus and later consul under Augustus 30 BC.

King of Numidia (r. 202-148 BC), Roman ally; the type-figure of the scrupulous foreign king. His prefect once carried off ivory from the Maltese Juno temple, and Masinissa returned it with an inscribed apology.
Slave or freedman of Cicero's, sent ahead to attend Tiro during his illness (Fam. 16.13).
Greek silversmith of the 4th century BC, the most famous embosser of his age. The thericlian cups attributed to him at Diodorus the Maltese's house were Verres's particular obsession.
Consul 60 BC; husband of Clodia (Catullus's Lesbia); died suddenly in 59 BC.
Tribune 62 BC (blocked Cicero's farewell consular speech); consul 57 BC (withdrew opposition to Cicero's recall); governor of Hither Spain 56--55 BC.
Greek rhetorician (c. 145-70 BC), of Asia Minor; famous for his prodigious memory. Antonius heard him as proconsul on the way to Cilicia.
Tribune 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; killed Clodius in the affray on the Appian Way in 52 BC; defended (unsuccessfully) by Cicero in Pro Milone.
Roman testator who died intestate before Verres's praetorship; Verres edicted to overturn the gens-inheritance in favour of a paid claimant.
King of Pontus (r. 120-63 BC); waged three wars against Rome 88-63 BC. In 88 BC he occupied the province of Asia and ordered the massacre of Romans (the Asiatic Vespers).
Consul-elect for 62 BC; defendant in Pro Murena (late November 63 BC) on charges of canvassing brought under Cicero's own bribery law.
Athenian sculptor of the early 5th century BC, famous for the Discobolus and the bronze heifer; the bronze Hercules in Heius's shrine and the bronze heifer at Athens were attributed to him.
Roman epic and dramatic poet (c. 270-201 BC); author of the Bellum Punicum and several tragedies and comedies.
Verres's runner and standing malicious prosecutor; convicted under C. Sacerdos for wrong-doing, then revived by Verres to file dummy claims under the name of Erucine Venus.
Last king of Bithynia (r. c. 94-74 BC); friend of Rome who bequeathed his kingdom to Rome at his death.
Wealthy Centuripine farmer who fled Sicily to Rome to escape Verres; condemned in absence of seven thousand medimni of wheat under the rigged tithe-recoverer system.
Agrigentine whose censer Verres took.
Roman testator who had not been registered in the census, so was unconstrained by the lex Voconia; left his only daughter as heir. Verres edicted retroactively to overturn the will.
Praetor 74 BC, colleague of Verres in supervising the patching of sacred buildings.
Verres's quaestor in Sicily; appealed to the praetor against the Syracusan senate's vote to revoke Verres's praise.
Recipient of the recommendation Fam. 13.51; otherwise unattested in the corpus.
Younger son of M. Licinius Crassus; Cicero's literary admirer; cavalry officer with Caesar in Gaul; killed at Carrhae in 53 BC alongside his father.
Twice senior officer of the principal tax-farming partnership in Africa; a friend whom Cicero indulges with serial recommendation letters in Fam. 13.6 and 13.6a.
Defendant in Pro Tullio; admitted (through his counsel L. Quinctius) that his armed slaves had attacked the plaintiff's establishment.
Senator on the original album of jurors for the Verres trial; rejected by Verres in the reiectio.
Roman citizen of Consa whom Verres had crucified at Messana facing Italy; his cry "civis Romanus sum" is the architectural climax of In Verrem II.5.
Roman contractor for the maintenance of the temple of Castor under the consuls 80 BC (Sulla and Metellus Pius); died leaving an infant son and ward, whom Verres targeted for plunder.
Urban quaestor with L. Triarius 84 BC, before whom Verres rendered his quaestorian accounts. (Probably the future Catilinarian conspirator P. Lentulus Sura, although the identification is debated.)
Roman consul 57 BC; the chief mover of Cicero's recall from exile. The Lentulus of the Fam. 1 correspondence.
Roman knight, otherwise unattested; commended in Fam. 13.51 to P. Caesius.

entities.canonical_name: Publius Mucius Scaevola and Lucius Calpurnius Piso Frugi (consuls 133 BC)
entities.summary: Consuls of 133 BC, in whose year the Sibylline books were resorted to and a senatorial embassy went to Henna to appease Ceres after the killing of Tiberius Gracchus.
Roman consuls 132 BC; with Rupilius they suppressed the First Sicilian Servile War, after which Rupilius gave Sicily its provincial charter.
The defendant in Cicero's first surviving speech (81 BC); brother and heir of Gaius Quinctius; embroiled in a partnership dispute with Sextus Naevius.
Roman consul 132 BC; suppressed the First Servile War in Sicily and afterwards settled Sicilian provincial law by the lex Rupilia (132 BC), the operating charter of Sicilian justice in Cicero's day.
Roman consul 105 BC, austere Stoic; condemned in 92 BC for repetundae as legate in Asia, despite his probity. The locus classicus of judicial injustice.
Roman knight who repeated Rubrius's sponsion against Apronius for 5,000 sesterces; Verres compelled him to pay Apronius the wager-sum as a reward.
Creditor of Gaius Quinctius; on his death the debt to his children had to be settled by Publius Quinctius, the Scapula children handled by Aquilius's good offices.

entities.canonical_name: Publius Servilius and Gaius Antistius (magistri publicanorum)
entities.summary: Joint masters of the Sicilian scriptura publicans whose letters Cicero produced at the trial.
Roman consul 79 BC; campaigned in Cilicia and Isauria 78-74 BC, taking Olympus, Corycus, and other pirate strongholds; triumphed 74 BC. A juror at Verres's trial.
Roman orator and tribune of the plebs 88 BC; an interlocutor in De Oratore as a young man. Killed in the Marian-Sullan strife in 88 BC.
Roman knight at Athens to whom 400,000 sesterces of Verres's slush funds were counted out, on Cicero's reckoning.
Senator who served on Nero's staff in Asia 79 BC; testified at the actio prima about the Lampsacum affair.
Guardian of the ward Iunius; testified under oath at the actio prima.
Wealthy Roman testator who instituted his freedman among his heirs, with the obligation to share half with his proscribed brother Aulus.
Roman knight, Verres's brother-in-law (his sister was Verres's wife) and Verres's quaestor's brother; testified against Verres in his own letters by the publicans.
Cicero's friend and guest-friend at Lilybaeum; victim of Verres's plundering of his ancestral hydria by Boethus.
Roman knight kept on Verres's consilium for graver matters; representative of the old equestrian discipline (Cassian-style strict justice).
Mamertine family made Roman citizens by Pompey, hence renamed Pompeii; gave lodging to Cicero's brother Lucius at Messana.
Athenian statesman and orator (c. 495-429 BC); leader of Athens in its greatest age; pupil of Anaxagoras.
Leading Ephesian summoned to Rome to answer for the obstruction of Aurelius Scaurus's quaestorian authority.
Last king of Macedon (r. 179-168 BC); defeated and captured at Pydna by Aemilius Paulus, ending the Antigonid dynasty.

Tyrant of Acragas (Agrigentum) c. 570-554 BC; proverbial cruelty-figure for the bronze bull in which he roasted men alive. Scipio Aemilianus restored the bull to the Agrigentines after taking Carthage.
Eloquent and prudent Herbitan, noble at home; gave the public testimony of his city against Verres.
King of Macedon (r. 221-179 BC); defeated by Flamininus at Cynoscephalae in the Second Macedonian War.

Mythological hero of the Trojan cycle, abandoned on Lemnos with a festering wound; subject of Sophocles's surviving tragedy and of two lost Roman tragedies, Accius's and Pacuvius's. A figure for the wronged man who watches the spectacles that grieve him.
Leading citizen of Lampsacum; defended his daughter against Verres's attempted rape and was condemned and executed at Laodicea on Dolabella's pressure.

Citizen of Opus condemned in absence in Cn. Dolabella's hearing; the precedent (when the Dolabella case was reopened) for rescinding Verres's condemnation of Sthenius. Distinct from the Lampsacene Philodamus.
Greek Epicurean philosopher and erotic poet (c. 110-c. 35 BC); resident in Piso's household. His epigrams survive in the Greek Anthology, his prose works on the Herculaneum papyri.
Freedman of Terentia; chief manager of Cicero's household business and accounts in the late 50s and through the civil war.
Leading Centuripine; charged by Verres with searching out the silver of his city. Witness at the Verres trial.
Wife of Atticus from c. 56 BC; mother of Pomponia (Caecilia Attica). A frequent dinner-partner in the late-50s correspondence.

Wife of the Syracusan Aeschrio; long-running Verres mistress; verses about her were posted above his tribunal at Syracuse. Her husband was set up as the standing publican of the Herbitan tithes for her benefit.
Tyrant of Athens (c. 600-527 BC); credited with first arranging Homer's books in their canonical order.
Consul 58 BC, father-in-law of Caesar; the other of the "two whirlwinds" of Pro Sestio (with Gabinius); proconsul of Macedonia and target of In Pisonem (55 BC).
Athenian philosopher (c. 428-348 BC), pupil of Socrates, founder of the Academy.
Roman comic playwright (c. 254-184 BC); twenty surviving plays, the great body of early Latin comedy.
Tyndaritan legate publicly in charge of the Mercury's removal to Messana.
Murgentine farmer beaten in Verres's bedroom and forced to accept a tripled tithe assessment.
Argive sculptor of the 5th century BC; author of the bronze Canephori (basket-bearing maidens) in Heius's shrine.
The general (106–48 BC); consul 70, 55, 52 BC; conqueror of Mithridates; member of the First Triumvirate; defeated at Pharsalus and murdered in Egypt.
Leading man of Solus, witness at the Verres trial.
Thermitan envoy at the Syracuse tithe-auction.
Roman knights through whom Verres laundered the bribes that bought him the praetorship; their books recorded the receipts which Verres's omitted.
Athenian sculptor of the 4th century BC; the Cupid in Heius's shrine at Messana was attributed to him, the same artist as the famous Thespian Cupid that L. Mummius spared.
Reader (lector) attached to M. Marius; mentioned in Fam. 7.1 as a man who could be put to read anything but Cicero's speeches aloud.
King of Egypt c. 80–58 and 55–51 BC; expelled in 58, restored by A. Gabinius's Roman force in 55. The Egyptian-restoration debate runs through the Spinther correspondence of 56.
King of Egypt; expelled by Alexandrian uprising 58 BC, restored to the throne by A. Gabinius's army from Syria in spring 55 BC; father of Cleopatra VII.
Praetor 56 BC, then proconsul of Macedonia 55–54 BC; recipient of Fam. 13.40 (a recommendation for the Aurelii) and Fam. 13.41–42.
Praetor 73 BC, defeated by Spartacus; expected by the Sicilians as the next praetor of Sicily, but never appointed governor (the post fell to Verres).
Praetor 79 BC, governor of Hispania Citerior 78-77 BC; convicted for extortion in 77 BC, prosecuted by Q. Lollius the younger.
Quintus Cicero's son and Cicero's nephew, born ca. 67 BC; later one of the assassins of Caesar (sometimes confused with M. Cicero the son).
Roman senator on the original Verres jury; let to be rejected by Verres for being too independent.
Senator on the Verres jury, an old comrade through whom Verres tried to substitute jurors of his own choosing.
Roman five-time consul (233-209 BC), dictator 217 BC; the famous Delayer who saved Rome by attrition against Hannibal. The historic owner of the silver horsemen Calidius held.
Roman senator on the original Verres jury; let to be rejected for the same reason.
Esteemed Roman knight nearly ninety years old, set as a spectacle at Apronius's banquet at Aetna. The Lollius episode was one of Cicero's most-remembered set pieces in the Verrines.

Other son of Q. Lollius; killed on his journey to Sicily, ostensibly by runaway slaves --- in fact (says Cicero) by Verres's agents, to keep his father's case from court. Had earlier prosecuted Q. Calidius for extortion.
Roman general and orator (149-87 BC), consul 102 with Marius, victor at Vercellae against the Cimbri. Father-in-law of Hortensius. Speaker on the second day of De Oratore.
Roman consul 102 BC, victor with Marius at Vercellae 101 BC; suicide 87 BC. Distinct from his son the consul 78 BC.
Roman consul 78 BC, son of the elder Catulus; conservative leader of the senate in the 70s and 60s; juror at the Verres trial.

entities.short_name: Catulus the elder of Cicero's day
entities.summary: Roman consul 78 BC, censor 65; \textit{princeps senatus} from c. 67. The chief honourer of Cicero on the Catilinarian conspiracy: gave him the title \textit{parens patriae} in the senate.
Sicilian made Roman citizen by Sulla on Catulus's recommendation; Verres took his citrus-wood table at Lilybaeum.
Roman consul 69 BC; subjugator of Crete (whence the cognomen). By his kindness Dio of Halaesa was made a Roman citizen and is hence styled Q. Caecilius Dion.
Roman consul 109 BC, victor over Jugurtha; refused to swear to Saturninus's agrarian law in 100 BC and went into exile rather than yield, returning the next year. The model of dignified retreat.
Sullan loyalist, consul 80 BC with Sulla; pontifex maximus; campaigner against Sertorius in Spain 79-72 BC.
Roman knight, defender of Sopater of Halicyae; left the bench in protest at Verres's dismissal of his consilium.

Roman jurist (c. 159-88 BC), consul 117, augur, son-in-law of Laelius and father-in-law of Crassus the orator. Speaker on the first day of De Oratore. To be distinguished from his cousin Q. Mucius Scaevola the Pontifex.

Roman consul 95 BC, pontifex maximus; the founding figure of Roman scientific jurisprudence. Killed in the Marian massacres 82 BC. The byword for an upright governor; Cicero contrasts his consilium with Verres's.
Senator and former tribune of the plebs; tried before Verres on a fictive charge under the lex Cornelia and stripped of all his fortunes in three hours.
The most celebrated Roman comic actor of his day (d. c. 62 BC); a friend of Cicero, who acknowledged learning oratorical delivery from him; defendant in Pro Roscio Comoedo.
Distinguished Roman knight whom Verres rewarded with a crown, trappings, and torque in a public meeting in Sicily. Distinct from Rubrius the procurer of the Lampsacum affair.
Roman knight whose resistance to Apronius's overcharge prompted Verres's spur-of-the-moment edict that grain not be removed from the threshing-floor before agreement with the tithe-collector.
Roman witness against Verres, related to Verres's mother's family; produced his books to show that he had given Verres money.
Roman senator on the Verres jury; brother of the witness Cn. Fannius.
Praetor 57 BC; propraetor of Africa Vetus 56 BC; later one of Caesar's commissioners settling veterans in Italy.
Witness at Verres's trial; said that Verres's agents took 130,000 sesterces from him for the administration of justice.
Tribune of the plebs 169 BC, author of the lex Voconia restricting women's inheritances by census-rated testators; cited by Cicero as prospective in form, unlike Verres's retroactive edict.
Cicero's younger brother (102–43 BC), praetor 62 BC, governor of Asia 61–58 BC, legate of Caesar in Gaul.
Aged senator defended by Cicero in Pro Rabirio Perduellionis Reo (63 BC), revived charge of perduellio for the killing of the tribune L. Saturninus 37 years earlier in 100 BC.
The greatest comic actor of late-Republican Rome (c. 126-62 BC), friend of Cicero, defended by him in 76 BC (Pro Roscio Comoedo); the Roman touchstone of perfect technique.
Verres's companion in the Lampsacum affair; the procurer who arranged the attempted rape of Philodamus's daughter.
Tribune 63 BC; proposer of the agrarian bill against which Cicero spoke the three De Lege Agraria speeches.
Thracian client-king visited by Verres on his Lampsacum journey, ostensibly on Roman business; the visit was a pretext for plunder.
Tribune 103 and 100 BC; killed by senatorial action under the senatus consultum ultimum in 100 BC, the precedent revived against Rabirius in 63 BC.
Roman general and statesman (185-129 BC), consul 147 and 134; conqueror of Carthage and Numantia. The central figure of De Re Publica.
Thessalian noble of Crannon, patron of Simonides; killed when his hall collapsed during a banquet — the catastrophe in which Simonides discovered the loci method.
Ptolemaic princess and Seleucid queen; mother of Antiochus XIII; the kings' claim to the kingdom of Egypt rested on her.
Roman matron, mother-in-law of Hortensius; old hostess of Dio of Halaesa, to whom Dio fled at Rome to complain of Verres. Distinct from the Servilia who was the mistress of Caesar.
Tribune 57 BC; champion of Cicero's recall from exile; defendant in Pro Sestio (March 56 BC) on charges of public violence; acquitted unanimously.
Quinctius's procurator at Rome and a relation of Sextus Naevius; tore down Naevius's notice of attachment and retook a slave Naevius had seized.
Henchman of P. Clodius (the tribune of 58); presided over the Compitalia of 1 January 58 BC against the senate's authority. Likely the author of much of the Clodian legislation.
Roman knight whom Verres dragged forth at a banquet at Messana, threw a cup at, and then had thrown into chains.
The antagonist in Pro Quinctio; a sometime auctioneer turned partner of Gaius Quinctius in Gaul; the speech accuses him of attempting to seize P. Quinctius's goods by procedural fraud.

Roman senator; let to be rejected from the Verres jury by Hortensius/Verres for being too independent in voting. Probably the son of the praetor 76 BC under whom Cicero had served as quaestor in Sicily.
Sicilian advocate of Dio's case, made Roman citizen for his virtue; one of Cicero's chief witnesses at the actio prima.
Defendant in Pro Roscio Amerino; son of the murdered Sex. Roscius the elder; charged with parricide.
Murdered father in Pro Roscio Amerino; first man of Ameria, friend of the Metelli, the Servilii, the Scipios; killed near the Pallacinian baths in 81 BC.
Verres's tithe-collector at Amestratus in the second year.
Lictor of Verres in Sicily; struck innocent men with the axe at Verres's bidding.
Athenian sculptor of the 4th century BC; author of the bronze Sappho in the Syracusan prytaneum carried off by Verres.
Consul-elect for 62 BC at the time of the Catilinarian debate; opened the senatorial discussion on 5 December 63 BC voting for the death penalty for the conspirators.
Greek lyric poet (c. 556-468 BC), credited as the inventor of the art of memory. The Scopas anecdote (Cic. De Or. 2.351-353) is the founding story of the loci-and-images mnemonic system.
Athenian philosopher (c. 470-399 BC), the founder of the philosophical-ethical tradition; condemned to death at Athens.

Leading citizen of Halicyae; acquitted under Sacerdos, then re-tried before Verres, who took 80,000 sesterces from him for acquittal, more from his prosecutors for condemnation, condemned him on the second day with the council dismissed.
Highest magistrate of Tyndaris; bound naked in winter rain to the equestrian statue of Marcellus by Verres for refusing to surrender the cult statue of Mercury. Distinct from Sopater of Halicyae.
Most eloquent Agrigentine, adorned with every learning and virtue; spoke before Pompey the consul on behalf of all Sicily about the wretchedness of the farmers.
Sicilian brothers of Agyrium from whose 22-year-old paternal inheritance Verres extorted nearly 400,000 sesterces under a Venus-fine clause.
Leading Entellan, sent as envoy with Artemo and Meniscus; offered to give grain free for the larder if the Sicilian assessment-rate was abolished.

entities.canonical_name: Sostratus, Numenius, and Nymphodorus (brothers of Centuripa)
entities.summary: Three Centuripine brothers in farming partnership; fled their fields under Apronius's exactions; Nymphodorus was hung from the wild olive in the Aetna forum at Apronius's order.
Roman critic and arbiter of plays selected for the public stage; the man whose taste oversaw the dramatic offerings at Pompey's games of 55 BC.
Freedman of Quintus Cicero, much trusted and (in Cicero's opinion) too prominent in the household. Recurring offstage figure in the Q.fr. correspondence.
Greek lyric poet of Himera (early 6th century BC); his bronze statue, an old man stooped with a book, was given by Scipio Aemilianus to the people of Thermae and coveted by Verres.
Citizen of Thermae in Sicily; his trial in absentia by Verres on a fabricated capital charge is the central narrative of In Verrem II.2.
The dictator (138–78 BC); marched on Rome twice; victor of the civil war of 83–82 BC; abdicated 79 BC. The proscriptions of his regime are the political background of Pro Roscio Amerino.
Nephew of the dictator; convicted of electoral bribery in 66 BC, defended by Cicero in Pro Sulla (62 BC) on a charge of complicity in the Catilinarian conspiracy.
Slave of Erucine Venus sent by Verres to Segesta as tithe-collector with letters of authorization to demand bail outside the proper forum.
Roman praetor c. 105 BC, governor of Sardinia; an Epicurean. Triumphed in Sardinia, then was condemned at Rome — Cicero's parallel for Piso.
Witness at the Verres trial on the Dio extortion.
Roman praetor who led colonists to Agrigentum out of the towns of the Sicilians by senatorial decree; date and identification disputed.
Roman consul 198 BC; defeated Philip V at Cynoscephalae 197 BC and proclaimed Greek freedom at the Isthmian Games 196 BC.
Cousin of the murdered Sex. Roscius; named by Cicero as one of the murderers and conspirators with Chrysogonus.
Cousin of the murdered Sex. Roscius; named by Cicero as one of the actual murderers and conspirators with Chrysogonus.
Cicero's first wife (c. 98–c. 6 BC), of independent property; mother of Tullia and Marcus the younger; divorced 46 BC.

Mime-actress's daughter, abducted by Verres from the Rhodian flute-player whose mistress she was; the controlling figure in Verres's Sicilian praetorship, said to have authority over him second only to Chelidon at Rome.
Lampsacene named in Verres's letter to Nero as one of the men who stirred up the people; never prosecuted.

entities.canonical_name: Theodorus, Numenius, and Nicasion of Henna
entities.summary: Hennensian envoys to the Verres trial; charged with demanding the return of Ceres and Victory.
Syracusan accomplice of Verres; co-conspirator in the Heraclius extortion and beneficiary of Verres's rigged appointment to the priesthood of Jupiter at Syracuse by triple-stuffed urn.
Greek philosopher (c. 371-287 BC), Aristotle's successor as head of the Peripatetic school; author of the Characters and a lost treatise On Style much used by Cicero.
Greek historian (c. 380-c. 320 BC), pupil of Isocrates; author of the lost Hellenica and Philippica.
Lampsacene named with Themistagoras as supposed instigator of the riot; never prosecuted.
Tyndaritan whom Verres robbed of a dish.
Athenian historian (c. 460-400 BC), author of the History of the Peloponnesian War; the model of dense and weighty prose for Cicero.
Roman tribune of the plebs 133 BC, killed in the popular reaction against his agrarian law; brother of Gaius Gracchus.
The unnamed Rhodian musician from whom Verres took Tertia by force.
Verres's freedman and chief broker of bribes in Sicily; managed the censorial sale-of-office at Syracuse and held more discretion than any provincial.

Cicero's slave, manumitted ca. 53 BC, secretary, literary collaborator and lifelong intimate; recipient of all letters in Ad Familiares book 16; later editor of Cicero's correspondence and posthumous biographer.
Cibyran-Greek painter, retainer of Verres, made a recoverer in the rigged Sicilian tithe-cases. With his brother Hiero, the chief Greek agent of Verres's art-plundering (a major figure in Book 4).
Cicero's beloved daughter (c. 79–45 BC); married three times (Piso, Crassipes, Dolabella); died after childbirth in February 45 BC, an event that crushed Cicero.
Chief man of his Sicilian city who took his own life under Verres's tithe regime.
Verres's herald in Sicily, made recoverer in tithe cases as part of the standing fix-the-jury combination with Volusius and Cornelius.
Tribune 59 BC; Caesarian; cross-examined by Cicero in In Vatinium (March 56 BC) as a hostile witness in the Sestius trial.
Verres's bidder against the Thermitans at the tithe-auction; the city had to pay him off to keep him from continuing to bid.
Praetor and propraetor of Sicily 73–71 BC; prosecuted by Cicero for extortion in 70 BC; withdrew into exile after the first action.
Roman knight, one of Verres's companions in the province; the launderer through whom bribes for the Sicilian inheritance suits passed.

Etruscan diviner attached to Verres's staff in Sicily; appointed as recoverer in tithe-cases despite having no qualification, in collusion with Apronius. A standing example of Verres's worthless consilium.
Noble of the Sicilian town of Menae; threatened with the rods to compel his wife's tithe.
Athenian historian and pupil of Socrates (c. 430-354 BC); author of the Anabasis, Hellenica, Memorabilia, Cyropaedia.
Tyndaritan envoys to the Verres trial; testified to the Mercury extortion.
Roman province covering most of mainland Greece south of Macedonia; created after Mummius's sack of Corinth in 146 BC.
The largest of the four cities of Syracuse, with the main forum, prytaneum, and Senate-house, and the temple of Olympian Jupiter.
The Ptolemaic kingdom; in the late 70s BC the kingdom whose succession was disputed at Rome.
Sicilian town at the foot of Mount Etna; site of the wild olive in the forum on which Apronius hung Nymphodorus, and of the dining-room Apronius spread for himself with Q. Lollius.
The Roman province of Africa (modern Tunisia and adjacent), administered from Utica; the western Mediterranean's chief grain region after Sicily.
Sicilian city on the south coast; site of the temple of Hercules whose statue Verres tried to remove in In Verrem II.4.
Sicilian town in the interior of the island, north of Henna; birthplace of the historian Diodorus Siculus.
Umbrian municipality; home of the Roscii in the case of Pro Roscio Amerino.
Inland Sicilian town; one of the cities forced to add cash gains to the tithe-buyers under Verres.
Capital of Seleucid Syria; birthplace of the poet Archias; centre of Greek culture in the eastern Mediterranean.
Coastal town south of Rome; site of one of Cicero's villas; setting of several Atticus letters.
Town on the Adriatic coast at the head of the Via Flaminia, near the Rubicon; sacked in the Sullan civil wars, an event Cicero invokes against Verres's accounts.
Roman province in western Anatolia, bequeathed by Attalus III in 133 BC; the wealthiest Roman possession of the late Republic and a perennial target of governors' extortion.
Major Pamphylian city on the Eurymedon river; renowned for its statues, all of which Verres carried off during his Cilician legateship.
Sicilian inland town near Henna; resisted Verres's plundering of the river-god Chrysas's shrine.
Ancient city in Attica, the cultural and intellectual centre of the Greek world; Cicero studied there in 79 BC.
Auction halls at Rome; Cicero invokes them in Pro Quinctio as the milieu of Naevius the auctioneer.
Fashionable seaside resort on the Bay of Naples; hot springs, villas, and (by Cicero's day) a reputation for moral laxity.
Town in Samnium on the Via Appia; Sulla settled supporters there and gave Verres confiscated property in its territory after Verres deserted Carbo.
Thracian people of the Rhodope mountain area; perpetually at war with Rome and her allies through Cicero's time.
Small Sicilian town not far from Syracuse, home of Epicrates whom Verres stripped of his goods on the Heraclius pattern.
Region of central Greece, north of Attica; reduced under Roman dominion by Mummius after the destruction of Corinth in 146 BC.
The principal port of southern Italy on the Adriatic; the embarkation and return point for Cicero's exile crossings.
Greek city on the Bosphorus, gatekeeping the Black Sea grain trade; a frequent target of profitable senatorial legations.
North Sicilian coastal town.
Town in southern Etruria; "the Capenate farm" is a hypothetical example of property in Cicero's mock formula at 2-2.31.
The chief hill of Rome, site of the temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus, where the consuls take vows on the Kalends of January.
Phoenician-founded city in north Africa, Rome's chief enemy in the Punic Wars; destroyed by Scipio Aemilianus in 146 BC, the spoils filling Italian and Sicilian temples.
Sicilian east-coast city at the foot of Mt. Etna.
Sicilian town in the interior north-east of Henna; faithful ally of Rome and one of the cities most active in pursuing Verres at his trial.
