Sicilian town on the north coast east of Panhormus; site of the famous shortening of the intercalary month under Verres's praetorship.
Aegean island off the Ionian coast, allied to Rome; sacked of statues by Verres on his Asian journey.
Phrygian city, home of the brothers Tlepolemus and Hiero whom Verres took into his service after they had fled charges of plundering the local Apolline temple.

Region of southern Anatolia bordering Syria; organized as a Roman province in the 70s BC, where Cn. Dolabella was governor 80-79 BC with Verres as his legate. Cicero himself governed Cilicia 51-50 BC.
The great chariot-racing arena in the valley between the Palatine and Aventine; the route of the pompa circensis from the Capitol to the Circus passed the statue of Vortumnus.
Carian coastal city famous for Praxiteles's marble Aphrodite (Venus), the canonical statue of the Hellenistic age.
Open-air assembly space at the north end of the Forum Romanum, in front of the Curia; used for legislative and judicial gatherings.
Capital of Hispania Ulterior, where L. Piso (praetor) had a goldsmith make him a ring in the open in the forum.
City on the Isthmus of Greece; sacked and destroyed by L. Mummius in 146 BC, the spoils filling Italian temples for generations.
Aegean island famous for Apelles's painted Aphrodite Anadyomene (the rival to the Cnidian Venus).
Aegean island reduced to a Roman province by Q. Caecilius Metellus Creticus 67 BC.
Greek city of Campania on the bay of Naples; site of Cicero's Cumae villa, much used as a writing retreat in the 50s.
Greek city on the Propontis; its Ajax and Medea were among the type-cases of the Greek civic-art treasures.
Aegean island sacred to Apollo, the god's mythical birthplace; site of the most ancient and revered Apolline shrine in the Greek world.
Coastal town in Hispania Citerior, stronghold of Sertorius; from Dianium to Sinope marked the entire Mediterranean range of pirate operations.
Sicilian harbour town just north of Lilybaeum; key naval anchorage in the western tip of the island.
Adriatic port city of Epirus, terminus of the Via Egnatia; Cicero's residence in late 58 BC and the city he sailed from on returning from exile.
Sicilian town in the interior; site of the temple of the Great Mother adorned by Scipio Aemilianus's spoils.
Inland Sicilian town in the western interior, of high labour and industry.
Major Ionian city on the western coast of Asia, site of the great temple of Artemis (one of the seven wonders) and of the asylum where slaves could take refuge.
Ionian city opposite Chios, plundered by Verres on his Asian journey.
Famous freshwater spring on Ortygia, mythological transformation of the nymph Arethusa.
Coastal town in southern Latium; site of one of Cicero's villas, and the place of his murder in December 43 BC.
The civic and judicial heart of Rome; site of the Rostra, the senatorial Curia, the basilicas where Cicero pleads.

The Roman province (Cisalpine, Transalpine, or both depending on context). In Pro Quinctio: the partnership's pasture land and Naevius's auction site; in the Caesarian letters: the theatre of the Gallic War.
The Roman territory in northern Italy between the Alps and the Apennines; Carbo's province as consul 84 BC, where Verres deserted him with the public money.
The Greek world as a whole, comprising the mainland (Achaia), the islands, and the coastal cities of Asia.
Sicilian town on the north coast, a free city by Roman charter; home of Dio, whose inheritance Verres extorted on the pretext that it had fallen to Erucine Venus.
Carian coastal city; site of the Mausoleum, one of the seven wonders. Statues plundered by Verres on his Asian journey.
Inland town in western Sicily, free city by Roman charter; home of Sopater.
Place or estate at issue in the Egnatius–Aquilius matter referenced in Att. 4.12; otherwise unknown.
North-coast Sicilian town set high on a steep climb; Verres remained at the seashore in his litter while Archagathus collected the silver from the town above.
The strait between the Aegean and the Propontis (modern Dardanelles); Lampsacum lay on its Asian shore.
Sicilian hill-town of the Ceres cult, in the centre of the island; site of the rape of Proserpine in myth, and of Verres's stripping of the temple of Ceres in In Verrem II.4.
Greek federate city in Lucania; the city in whose roll Archias was enrolled, the basis of his claim to Roman citizenship under the lex Plautia Papiria.
Sicilian town on the south coast, between Agrigentum and Selinus; one of the colonies refounded by P. Rupilius after the First Servile War. Distinct from Heraclea Lucania.
Sicilian inland town near Henna; one of the cities that publicly testified against Verres.
Sicilian Greek city destroyed by the Carthaginians in 409 BC; its surviving citizens settled at nearby Thermae. Birthplace of Stesichorus.
The two Roman Spanish provinces; assigned jointly to Pompey by the lex Trebonia of February 55 BC for his five-year proconsulship.
Suburban estate of Furius Crassipes outside Rome; a frequent dinner venue for Cicero in the mid-50s.
Mountain near Athens, by metonymy a sign of Athenian (Academic, Stoic) philosophical learning.
Sicilian town near the Symaethus river.
The Italian peninsula; in Cicero's letters of the civil war years synonymous with the homeland Pompey's army left behind in 49 BC.
Island in the Ionian Sea, kingdom of Odysseus; the figure of beloved homeland in Homer.
Greek city on the Asian shore of the Hellespont; site of Verres's most notorious legateship outrage, the attempted rape of Philodamus's daughter and the consequent execution of father and son.
Phrygian city; seat of the Roman governor's assize for the Cibyratic conventus, where Philodamus and his son were executed by Dolabella's order.
Town in Samnium (modern Larino), famous as the home of the defendant in Pro Cluentio.
Sicilian inland city north-west of Syracuse; reduced and impoverished, but threw down Verres's statue in the gymnasium.
Westernmost Sicilian harbour town; one of the four Sicilian conventus seats and the standing post of the western quaestor. Cicero himself had been quaestor at Lilybaeum 75 BC.
The chief of the Aeolian Islands off northern Sicily.
Coastal lagoon between Cumae and Puteoli, famed for its oyster beds and luxury villas.
Region on the southern coast of Anatolia, west of Pamphylia; harassed by Verres's grain-levies and arbitrary valuations.
The kingdom of Philip and his successors, conquered by Aemilius Paullus at Pydna 168 BC and turned into a Roman province in 146 BC.
The island of Malta, dependency of Sicily; home of Diodorus and site of the temple of Juno on its promontory which Masinissa had restored and Verres plundered.
City on the strait between Sicily and Italy; Verres's collaborators' city, and the site of the cross facing Italy on which Cicero's Roman citizen Publius Gavius was hung.
Major Ionian city at the mouth of the Maeander; provided one of ten ships for the Roman fleet against pirates, which Verres sold off to enemies.
Mountainous region on the borders of Lycia, Pisidia, and Phrygia; harassed by Verres as deputy quaestor under Dolabella.
Volcanic massif south-east of Rome with many luxury villas; site of Pompey's Alban estate.
Mountain in western Sicily crowned by the great sanctuary of Venus Erycina; revenue and ornaments of the temple were a perennial pretext for Verres's confiscations.
Sicilian town in the south-eastern interior, in the Hyblaean hills.
Carian coastal town near Halicarnassus; Verres sold there the Milesian ship that had been given him as escort.
Principal city of Lesbos; another conventional place of cultivated Roman exile.
Roman colony in Transalpine Gaul (founded 118 BC); in Pro Quinctio, the proposed site for an auction Quinctius was dissuaded from holding.
Greek-foundation city of Campania; home of L. Papirius Paetus and a haunt of Cicero's literary set in the 50s and 40s.
Fourth quarter of Syracuse, named "new town" because last built; site of the largest Greek theatre and of the temples of Ceres and Libera and the great image of Apollo Temenites.
Sicilian town in the south-east, free city by Roman charter.
Pirate stronghold in eastern Lycia; taken by P. Servilius Vatia in his Isaurian campaigns 78-74 BC.
Town in eastern Locris, central Greece.
The original urban core of Syracuse, an island connected to the mainland by a causeway; the praetorian palace, the temples of Diana and Minerva, and the spring of Arethusa lay there.
Region on the southern coast of Anatolia; under loose Roman control in the 70s BC, particularly through the campaigns of Servilius and the legateship of Dolabella.
North Sicilian harbour city, conventus seat in Cicero's day.
Pamphylian city, site of an ancient and venerable shrine of Diana, stripped of its gold by Verres.
The Achaemenid empire; in Cicero's argument the touchstone of impiety, since even the Persian fleet (he says) refrained from sacking Delos when invading Greece.
Sicilian town; Cicero charges that Verres extorted 52,000 sesterces from the Petrines under Naevius Turpio.

Lycian coastal town taken by P. Servilius Vatia 76 BC; before then a Greek city, became a pirate haven by accident of geography. Cicero uses it as the type-case for the Mamertine relation to Verres's brigandage.
Coastal Sicilian town at the mouth of the Himera; one of the standard delivery points for tithe-grain.
Inland region of western Anatolia, attached to the province of Asia; harassed by Verres as deputy quaestor.
Mountainous region of central Anatolia; harassed by Verres's grain-levies as deputy quaestor under Dolabella.
Town in Cisalpine Gaul, modern Piacenza; an Insubrian colony of the second century BC. Piso's maternal grandfather's town.
Town on the bay of Naples, on the slope of Vesuvius; site of one of Cicero's villas, the Pompeianum, where he stayed before joining Pompey in 49 BC.
The first permanent stone theatre at Rome, built by Pompey in the Campus Martius and dedicated in 55 BC; the games of Fam. 7.1 inaugurated it.
Kingdom on the southern coast of the Black Sea, ruled by Mithridates VI; the eastern terminus of pirate range from Dianium in Spain.
The chief Roman port for the eastern trade, on the bay of Naples just north-west of the Lucrine lake; a centre of commerce and gossip.
Greek-Italian city on the Calabrian shore opposite Messana; a refuge for those fleeing Sicilian governors.
Aegean island; rhetorical and philosophical centre; conventional place of polite Roman exile in the Greek east.
The capital. Almost every speech is delivered here; almost every letter is sent from or to here.
Aegean island close to the Ionian coast; site of the great Heraion, sacked of statues by Verres in his Asian legateship.
Mediterranean island; Roman province from 238 BC, governed jointly with Corsica.
Sicilian city in the west; site of the bronze Diana whose theft by Verres is one of the famous narrative passages of In Verrem II.4.
The chamber where the Senate met; on the north side of the Forum.
Temple of Serapis in Syracuse, in the most frequented quarter of the city; before its vestibule the multitude tore down Verres's statues.
First Roman province (from 241 BC); subject of In Verrem; its cities supplied much of Rome's grain.
Greek city west of Corinth; Verres demanded money of its magistrate and tortured him with green-wood smoke when he refused.
The bronze statue of the Etruscan god Vortumnus at the corner of the Vicus Tuscus where it entered the Forum Romanum; a landmark on the route of triumphs and processions toward the Circus Maximus.
Greek city on the southern Black Sea coast, capital of the Pontic kingdom; the eastern terminus of pirate range.
North Sicilian town east of Panhormus; home of Posides Macro.
Coastal town south of Pompeii, looking onto the Stabian gulf; M. Marius's villa had a window cut to its view.
Capital of Roman Sicily; centre of Verres's depredations; site of the Achradina executions and the Latomiae quarry-prison in In Verrem II.5.
Roman province organised by Pompey in 64 BC; assigned to Crassus by the lex Trebonia of February 55 BC for his five-year proconsulship.
A money-changer's table or bank in the Forum; the spot where Naevius staged his witness-testifying that Quinctius had not appeared.
Greek city of southern Italy; its Europa-on-the-bull and Vesta-temple Satyr were among the famous local statues Cicero invokes.
East Sicilian town on the slopes of Mt. Etna; a federate city under treaty with Rome (one of three such in the island), and yet threw down Verres's statue.
Temple in the Forum, used in Cicero's day for public business and as a checking point for treasury account-balances; mentioned in Pro Quinctio §17.
Temple at the foot of the Capitoline beside the Forum; site of the Senate meeting of 5 December 63 BC at which Cicero delivered the Fourth Catilinarian.
Roman temple in the Velabrum, where the Thespiads (the Muses) brought back by L. Mummius from Thespiae were dedicated.
Roman temple at the Porta Capena; the spoils Marcellus brought from Syracuse 212 BC were dedicated there. (Marcellus had vowed two temples on taking the city.)
Temple at the foot of the Palatine, near the start of the Sacra Via; site of the Senate meeting of 8 November 63 BC at which Cicero delivered the First Catilinarian.
Aegean island near the entrance of the Hellespont, said to be founded by the hero Tenes; Verres carried off Tenes's cult statue.
Town on the north coast of Sicily, refounded after Carthaginian destruction of nearby Himera; home of Sthenius, and the only town in the province from which Verres could carry off no public statue.
Boeotian town, famous for Praxiteles's marble Cupid; Mummius spared it as consecrated.
Capital of Macedonia; Cicero's residence for most of his exile in 58 BC, in the household of the quaestor Plancius.
Small Sicilian town of painstaking and thrifty farmers; harassed by the Venusian slave Diognetus.
Underground state prison near the Forum; place of execution by strangling for state criminals; site of the killing of Lentulus, Cethegus, and the other Catilinarians on the night of 5 December 63 BC.
Hill town near Rome; site of Cicero's favourite villa and the setting of the Tusculan Disputations.
Third quarter of Syracuse, named for an old shrine of Fortune; site of the great gymnasium.
North Sicilian town founded by Dionysius I; the Tyndaritans threw down Verres's statue and left the empty horse in the forum as a stronger reproach.
Phoenician-founded coastal city north-west of Carthage in the Roman province of Africa; M. Fabius Hadrianus was burnt alive in his praetorium there 82 BC.
Coastal location in Etruria; the place where Quinctius was sighted by Publicius travelling to Gaul on 29 January 83 BC.
Etruscan town; site of Sulla's camp during the closing campaign of the civil war (81 BC).
Cicero's lost autobiographical hexameter poem in three books on his consulship, exile and recall, completed ca. 55 BC; the speech of Jupiter mentioned in Q.fr. 2.7 closed its third book.
Lost Roman tragedy by Lucius Accius, on the murder of Agamemnon; revived at Pompey's games of 55 BC, with six hundred mules in the procession.

Lost Roman tragedy (probably Naevius's), on the fall of Troy by the wooden horse; revived at Pompey's games of 55 BC, with three thousand mixing-bowls (creterrarum tria milia) in its triumphal pageant.
Platonic dialogue on love and rhetoric, set under a plane-tree by the Ilissus stream outside Athens. The model for the Tusculan setting of De Oratore.
Tragedy of the Greek hero Philoctetes; Sophocles's survives, Accius's and Pacuvius's are lost. Cicero's allusion in Q.fr. 2.8 is to the figure rather than to a specific dramatic version.

Banker of Puteoli, Atticus's and Cicero's correspondent on the bay of Naples; a recurring background figure of the 50s and 40s letters, conduit for messages between the brothers and their friends in town.
The greatest Roman polymath of the late Republic, antiquarian, grammarian, satirist, and historian. Cicero draws on Varro's books for De Re Publica; later dedicates the Academica to him.
Consul 182 and 168 BC; victor over Perseus of Macedon at Pydna in 168 BC. Cicero's exemplum of dignified paludatum departure: the type of the Roman commander setting out for war in proper form.

Caesar's bitter and unfortunate colleague as consul in 59 BC, driven to retreat to his house and lodge religious objections from indoors. Optimate stalwart; one of the four senators who voted with Cicero on the Tenedian deputation in early 54 BC.
Praetor 57 BC, orator of the Atticist school praised by Cicero in Brutus 274–278; one of the four who voted with Cicero on the Tenedian deputation in early 54 BC.
Tribune 52 BC, Catonian Stoic and pungent senatorial speaker, often called Cato's ape for his manner. One of the four who voted with Cicero on the Tenedian deputation in early 54 BC.
Roman acting in Asia, evidently a publican or a pro-magistrate's officer, who lodged some demand against the Magnesians from Sipylum; resisted in the senate by Quintus Cicero. Otherwise unknown.

Roman poet of the late Republic, author of the De Rerum Natura, the Epicurean cosmological poem in six books. Cicero's brief judgement in Q.fr. 2.9 — many flashes of genius, much craft also (text disputed) — is the earliest known reception.
Friend of Cicero and Atticus; not the historian. Author of the Empedoclea, a turgid Empedoclean cosmological poem in hexameters that the brothers Cicero teased each other for trying to read.
Young Roman jurist, Cicero's protégé, recommended to Caesar in 54 BC; recipient of the Fam. 7 letter-string. The leading Roman jurist of the next generation, dedicatee of Horace's Satires 2.1.

Spanish-Roman of Gades, Caesar's effective chief of staff and intermediary, defended by Cicero, Pompey, and Crassus on his Roman citizenship in 56 BC (Pro Balbo). Cicero's main channel for advancing Trebatius's career in Caesar's camp in 54 BC.
Roman of the equestrian or senatorial class, candidate for a tribunate of the soldiers in 53 BC, on whose behalf Cicero approached the consul Domitius. Otherwise unidentified.

Consul 54 BC, optimate stalwart and brother-in-law of Cato; long-standing enemy of Caesar; commander of Corfinium, captured and released, killed at Pharsalus in 48 BC. Famously stingy with petty patronage during his consulship.

Small island off the Troad in the Aegean, near Troy. The Tenedian axe — proverbially sharp — gave Cicero his pun in Q.fr. 2.9 on the senate's refusal of the Tenedian embassy's request for a renewed grant of liberty.

Greek city of Lydia at the foot of Mt Sipylus (modern Manisa, Turkey); free city in the Roman period. Subject of an embassy to Rome in early 54 BC at which Quintus Cicero was praised for resisting L. Sestius Pansa.

The island of Britain, target of Caesar's two expeditions in 55 and 54 BC. Cicero's letters of 54 carry the joke that there was no gold or silver to plunder there, and his pledge to write a poem on the campaign.

A poem in Empedoclean style, presumably on cosmological themes after the Greek Pre-Socratic, by Cn. Sallustius (friend of Cicero, not the historian). Lost; known only from Cicero's tease at Q.fr. 2.9.3.
